original_abstract: Utilizing high-resolution mass spectrometry, we investigated the synthesis of isoquinoline within charged electrospray droplets and the complexation of cytochrome c with maltose during droplet fusion. Our objective was to evaluate the capacity of microdroplets to catalyze both covalent and non-covalent interactions at rates exceeding those found in conventional bulk solutions. In both scenarios, we observed dramatic reaction acceleration: a million-fold increase for isoquinoline synthesis and a thousand-fold increase for protein-ligand complexation. These findings suggest that conducting reactions within microdroplets (approximately 1–15 μm in diameter) serves as a robust general method for enhancing kinetic rates. While the exact underlying mechanism remains to be fully elucidated, reagent confinement and droplet evaporation appear to be primary drivers. Notably, in experiments involving fused aqueous droplets, evaporation was negligible during the flight time between fusion and analysis. This indicates that evaporation is not the sole driver of acceleration in aqueous systems, suggesting instead that the droplet–air interface plays a critical role. We propose that "microdroplet chemistry" represents a powerful alternative for driving sluggish or difficult reactions and, when paired with mass spectrometry, offers a sophisticated platform for exploring chemical and biochemical processes in highly confined environments.
humanized_abstract: We investigated using high-resolution mass spectrometry the chemistry of charged species in an electrospray that resulted in the formation of isoquinoline or in the complexation of cytochrome c with maltose upon the fusion of droplets. These studies were performed with the goal of assessing the capabilities of microdroplets to act as catalysts in the formation of covalent bonds or non-covalent interactions that proceed with rates enhanced relative to those observed in solution phase under the same conditions. We found that in both examples the catalytic rates were enhanced by a millionfold relative to those in solution and by a thousandfold relative to those when cytochrome c is added to maltose solution. Hence, the chemistry occurring within the microdroplets (droplet diameters ranging from 1 to 15 μm) constitutes a robust and general method for the catalysis of rapid reaction formation. We discuss potential catalytic mechanisms associated with droplet confinement, as well as well as those arising from the effects of droplet evaporation. Because the evaporation in the fused aqueous droplets occurred on a timescale of seconds longer than their flight time in the spray, the evaporation between fusion and MS analysis does not appear to be sufficient to explain the enhanced catalysis of aqueous droplet fusions in the same manner that it dominates the chemistry in charged, air–water droplets and hydrophobic cavities. "Microdroplet chemistry" appears to provide a viable means of increasing the rates of catalytic reactions proceeding through rather slow or barriers steps of bonding as well as the binding of substrates and that, combined with its ability to produce detailed structural information with high mass resolution and precision, presents an outstanding tool for the comprehensive study of fast occurring biochemical reactions as well as fast occurring chemical reactions within confined environment.
